rounds.blurb: V-J Day in Times Square: a sailor's kiss as news of Japan's surrender breaks.
rounds.credit: Victor Jorgensen, U.S. Navy — public domain
rounds.story: When Japan's surrender was announced, Times Square erupted. Navy photographer Victor Jorgensen caught the same kiss as Eisenstaedt's famous LIFE frame from a different angle — and the government version is the one that belongs to everyone.

rounds.blurb: "Into the Jaws of Death": U.S. troops wade toward Omaha Beach on the morning of D-Day.
rounds.credit: Robert F. Sargent, U.S. Coast Guard — public domain
rounds.story: Coast Guard coxswain Robert Sargent shot this from the ramp of his landing craft at Omaha Beach on D-Day morning, delivering the 1st Infantry Division into machine-gun fire. By nightfall the Allies had put more than 150,000 men ashore.

rounds.blurb: The rings are forged: opening ceremony of the London Summer Olympics.
rounds.story: Danny Boyle's ceremony forged the Olympic rings from a staged Industrial Revolution, parachuted the Queen in alongside James Bond, and gave 600 dancing NHS nurses a global stage. Roughly a billion people watched.

rounds.blurb: The International Space Station begins: the Unity node rides in Endeavour's payload bay before mating with Zarya.
rounds.story: Endeavour's crew mated the American Unity node to Russia's Zarya module, the station's first two pieces. The ISS has been continuously occupied since 2000 — the longest unbroken human presence off Earth.

rounds.blurb: Test pilot Mike Melvill celebrates atop SpaceShipOne after the first privately funded human spaceflight.
rounds.story: Melvill rode SpaceShipOne past the edge of space on the first of the two flights that clinched the $10 million Ansari X Prize. It was the first privately built and funded craft to carry a human to space — the proof of concept behind commercial spaceflight.
